narrative_summary:
The CDH1 c.8C>G (p.Pro3Arg) missense change affects the first codon of the mature E‑cadherin protein and has been reported in a small number of individuals with early‑onset lobular breast cancer and in somatic tumours such as endometrial and ovarian carcinoma [PMC3003879][PMC6057103].

Clinical reports describe the variant in two unrelated patients with lobular breast cancer diagnosed before age 40, each with a family history of breast or other non‑gastric cancers but no documented gastric cancer in relatives [PMC3003879]. A later review of CDH1 germline missense mutations noted its presence among non‑gastric cancer cases, suggesting that CDH1 testing may be relevant beyond diffuse gastric cancer [PMC8151827]. In a large genotype‑first cohort, the same variant was classified as likely benign/benign because it showed no association with diffuse gastric or lobular breast cancer phenotypes [PMC9810541]. Conversely, a German diagnostic panel listed c.8C>G as a variant of uncertain significance in a breast‑cancer cohort [PMC5911592], and the Cenani‑Lenz syndactyly report merely recorded it as a somatic alteration in endometrial and ovarian tumours without interpretation [PMC6057103].

Functional assessment of the variant showed that it resides in the signal peptide and was predicted by SIFT to be tolerated, indicating a low likelihood of pathogenic impact [PMC3003879]. Experimental expression of the mutant protein in cells lacking endogenous E‑cadherin demonstrated normal membrane localisation, further supporting a benign effect, although the authors noted that subtle functional deficits could not be excluded [PMC3003879].

Computational prediction tools uniformly classified the amino‑acid substitution as tolerated, consistent with a non‑deleterious effect on protein function [PMC3003879]. No population allele‑frequency data were provided in the extracted sources.

Taken together, the preponderance of evidence—including benign‑leaning functional results, tolerant in‑silico predictions, and classification as likely benign in a large genotype‑first study—supports the interpretation of CDH1 c.8C>G as a likely benign or non‑pathogenic variant. However, the presence of the variant in a few early‑onset breast‑cancer cases and its designation as a VUS in some diagnostic panels reflect residual uncertainty, and further segregation or larger case‑control data would be needed to definitively exclude a modest risk association.

mentions.quoted_text: We did find 10 patients with non-synonymous variants. One non-synonymous change, c.1774G A, p.A592T, was found in two patients and is a known germline variant that is not associated with risk of familial breast cancer or HDGC. The variant, c.2494G A, p.V832M, which had previously been identified in a patient with HDGC and was functionally characterised as a pathogenic mutation, was found in a woman who was diagnosed as having LBC at the age of 43 years and had a family history of ductal breast cancer in a sister and unspecified breast cancer in a maternal aunt. Segregation analysis has not yet been performed. The remaining non-synonymous variants were novel and did not appear in any public databases. These variants were c.8C G, p.P3R; c.1223C T, p.A408V; c.1297G A, p.D433N; c.1813A G, p.R695G and c.88 C A, p.P30T, which were found in two patients not known to be related. There was no family history of gastric cancer for any of the patients who carried novel non-synonymous variants (table 1).

Non-synonymous variant Criteria 1 or 2 BRCA1/2 mutation status Age at diagnosis Family history (age at diagnosis) c.8C G, p.P3R 1 Negative 38 years Maternal aunt=breast cancer (46 years)Maternal aunt=breast cancer (67 years)Maternal cousin=breast cancer (42 years)Mother=retroperitoneal tumourPaternal grandmother=breast cancer c.88 C A, p.P30T (two patients) 1 Unknown 40 years 2 Negative 47 years Paternal aunt=breast cancer (40 years)Female paternal cousin=breast cancer (40 years)Male paternal cousin=breast cancer (50 years)Female paternal cousin=breast cancer (47 years) c.1223C T, p.A408V 1 Negative 44 years No cancers c.1297G A, p.D433N 1 Negative 41 years Paternal grandmother=intestinal cancerMaternal grandmother=lung cancerMaternal grandfather=mouth cancer c.1813A G, p.R605G 1 Unknown 42 years Mother=breast cancer (60 years)Maternal uncle=pancreatic cancer (64 years) c. 2494G A, p.V832M(known missense mutation in HDGC) 1 Negative 43 years Sister=ductal breast cancerMaternal aunt=breast cancerPaternal uncle=leukaemiaPaternal grandmother=colon cancer

We performed several tests to assess the likelihood that any of the non-synonymous variants resulted in a loss of normal function. Web-based software (Sorting Intolerant from Tolerant, SIFT) that predicts whether the amino acid change conferred by non-synonymous variants might alter protein structure, and thus possibly function, indicated that all but one variant, c.8C G, p.P3R, which occurred in the signal peptide of the pre-protein, should be tolerated and therefore is unlikely to be pathogenic. Moreover, web-based software (Berkeley Drosophila Genome Project, Splice Site Prediction by Neural Network, Berkeley, Calif) did not predict alteration of splicing by any of the novel synonymous or non-synonymous variants or intronic variants identified.

The likely pathogenicities of the novel non-synonymous variants were further assessed by analysing the predicted effects of amino acid changes on the three-dimensional structure of E-cadherin. Because the coordinates of the three-dimensional structure of the ectodomain of E-cadherin were not available, we used the model of the closely related paralog, C-cadherin, to predict likely changes in the structure. One of the mutations, c.1223C T, p.A408V, changes the alanine residue, which is well-conserved in this family of proteins, to bulkier valine and is located in calcium ion-binding extracellular domain 3. Surface modelling of the mutated protein indicated that this bulky valine could conceivably alter the binding pocket of one of three calcium ions that mediate homotypic cadherin domain interactions (Supplementary figure 1). Another mutation, c.1297G A, p.D433N, was also found to be located in close proximity to this calcium-binding site (Supplementary figure 1). Because the c.8C G, p.P3R variant occurs in the signal peptide of the precursor protein and had been predicted to be pathogenic, we hypothesised that this variant could result in mislocalisation or lack of expression of E-cadherin on the cell surface. To test this hypothesis, we expressed normal E-cadherin or each of the mutated versions of the protein in cells lacking endogenous E-cadherin. As seen in Supplementary figure 2, E-cadherin mutated with the c.8C G, p.P3R variant did exhibit membrane localisation, indicating that protein localisation was not grossly affected by this variant. Additionally, the other novel non-synonymous variants also demonstrated membrane localisation (data not shown). However, because the levels at which we expressed E-cadherin were not physiological, it is possible that subtle effects of the mutations could have been missed.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The CDH1 c.8C>G (p.P3R) variant is a rare missense change identified in a few early‑onset lobular breast cancer patients, with computational and functional data suggesting benignity but the authors’ overall assessment classifying it among potentially pathogenic variants, leaving its clinical significance uncertain.
article_mentions.title: Germline mutations in CDH1 are infrequent in women with early-onset or familial lobular breast cancers

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The article describes a Cenani‑Lenz syndactyly family caused by a homozygous LRP4 mutation; the CDH1 c.8C>G variant is only mentioned in a supplementary table without any interpretation related to the disease.
article_mentions.title: Cenani-Lenz syndactyly syndrome - a case report of a family with isolated syndactyly

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The review reports that CDH1 germline missense mutations, including c.8C>G, are observed in non‑gastric cancers such as breast cancer, supporting consideration of CDH1 testing beyond gastric cancer.
article_mentions.title: Frequency of CDH1 Germline Mutations in Non-Gastric Cancers

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The genotype‑first study found that truncating CDH1 pathogenic/likely‑pathogenic variants are strongly associated with diffuse gastric and lobular breast cancer, whereas missense variants of unknown significance, including c.8C>G, show no such association and c.8C>G is classified as likely benign/benign in the supplementary data.
article_mentions.title: Genotype-first approach to identify associations between CDH1 germline variants and cancer phenotypes: a multicentre study by the European Reference Network on Genetic Tumour Risk Syndromes
